subject: ap-chemistry
title: Urea: Structure, Solubility, Entropy & Thermodynamics
stimulus:
The compound urea, H
NCONH
, is widely used in chemical fertilizers. The complete Lewis electron-dot
diagram for the urea molecule is shown above.

parts.prompt: Identify the hybridization of the valence orbitals of the carbon atom in the urea molecule.
parts.scoring_notes: In urea (H$_2$N−CO−NH$_2$), the carbon atom forms: 1 double bond to O (counts as 1 electron domain), 2 single bonds to N = 3 total electron domains around carbon → sp$^2$ hybridization. The double bond C=O uses one p orbital for the π bond; carbon is sp$^2$.

scoring_points.description: For identifying sp$^2$ hybridization of the carbon atom in urea: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: $sp^2$. 1 point is earned for the correct answer.

sample_responses.response: The carbon atom in urea forms three electron domains: a double bond to O (counts as 1 domain) and two single bonds to N. Three electron domains → $sp^2$ hybridization.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: correctly identifies three electron domains around C (one C=O, two C−N) leading to $sp^2$.

sample_responses.response: The carbon has four bonds (C=O and two C−N and lone pair), so it is $sp^3$.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point: a double bond counts as ONE electron domain, not two. Carbon in urea has 3 electron domains → $sp^2$, not $sp^3$.

alternatives.correct_example: The carbon atom in urea forms 3 electron domains: a double bond to O (counts as 1 domain) and 2 single bonds to N. Three electron domains → sp$^2$ hybridization.

wrong_examples: sp$^3$ — only applies when carbon has 4 single bonds or 4 electron domains. The C=O double bond reduces domains to 3.
sp — only when carbon has 2 electron domains (as in a triple bond or two double bonds).
common_traps: Count electron domains (bonding + lone pairs), NOT bond count. A double bond = 1 electron domain. C in urea: 1 (C=O) + 1 (C−N) + 1 (C−N) = 3 domains → sp$^2$.

parts.prompt:
Urea has a high solubility in water, due in part to its ability to form hydrogen bonds. A urea molecule and
four water molecules are represented in the box below. Draw ONE dashed line (----) to indicate a possible
location of a hydrogen bond between a water molecule and the urea molecule.
→
H NCONH (s) ← H NCONH (aq)
2 2 2 2
The dissolution of urea is represented by the equation above. A student determines that 5.39 grams of
H NCONH (molar mass 60.06 g/mol) can dissolve in water to make 5.00 mL of a saturated solution at 20.°C.
parts.scoring_notes: A hydrogen bond between urea and water can form between: (1) an N−H of urea and the lone pair of O in water (H−N···O), or (2) the O=C of urea (lone pair on O) and the H−O of a water molecule (H−O···O=C). Any one valid H-bond drawn with a dashed line between H and an electronegative atom (N or O) earns the point.

parts.prompt: Calculate the concentration of urea, in mol/L, in the saturated solution at 20.°C.
parts.scoring_notes: Moles urea = 5.39 g / 60.06 g/mol = 0.08974 mol. Volume = 5.00 mL = 0.00500 L. Molarity = 0.08974 mol / 0.00500 L = 17.9 M.

scoring_points.description: For the correct moles of urea: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: $5.39\text{ g H}_2\text{NCONH}_2 \times \frac{1\text{ mol}}{60.06\text{ g}} = 0.0897\text{ mol}$. 1 point is earned for the correct number of moles of urea (may be implicit).

sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: correct division of mass by molar mass.
Does not earn the point: multiplied instead of dividing by molar mass.

wrong_examples: Not dividing by molar mass — must convert grams to moles first.
common_traps: Moles = mass / molar mass. Molar mass of urea (H$_2$NCONH$_2$) = 60.06 g/mol.

scoring_points.description: For the correct molarity = 17.9 M: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: $\frac{0.0897\text{ mol}}{0.00500\text{ L}} = 17.9\ M$. 1 point is earned for the correct molarity.

sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: correctly converts 5.00 mL to 0.00500 L and divides moles by liters.
Does not earn the point: volume must be in liters (0.00500 L), not mL.

wrong_examples: Dividing by 5.00 mL directly instead of 0.00500 L — volume must be in liters.
Dividing mol by the mass of urea — denominator must be volume in liters.
common_traps: Molarity = moles / volume(L). Convert 5.00 mL → 0.00500 L before dividing.

parts.prompt:
The student also determines that the concentration of urea in a saturated solution at 25°C is 19.8 M. Based
on this information, is the dissolution of urea endothermic or exothermic? Justify your answer in terms of
Le Chatelier's principle.
parts.scoring_notes: Endothermic. The solubility increases from 17.9 M (20°C) to 19.8 M (25°C) when temperature increases. By Le Chatelier's principle, increasing temperature shifts the equilibrium in the direction that absorbs heat. If higher temperature increases solubility, the dissolution reaction must be absorbing heat (endothermic) to shift toward more dissolved urea.

scoring_points.description: For 'endothermic' with Le Chatelier's reasoning from the solubility increase with temperature: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: The increased solubility at the higher temperature implies that the dissolution of urea is endothermic. If a saturated solution of urea is heated, then the equilibrium system is stressed. The stress is counteracted by the endothermic dissolution of more urea. 1 point is earned for the correct answer with an appropriate justification.

sample_responses.response: Endothermic. Increasing temperature from 20°C to 25°C increases the solubility from 17.9 M to 19.8 M. By Le Chatelier's principle, a temperature increase shifts equilibrium in the endothermic direction (absorbs heat). Since more urea dissolves at higher temperature, the dissolution is endothermic.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: correct direction (endothermic) with valid Le Chatelier reasoning tied to the observed increase in solubility.

sample_responses.response: Exothermic, because dissolving releases heat to the water and warms the solution.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point: if dissolution were exothermic, increasing temperature would DECREASE solubility by Le Chatelier's principle. Since solubility increases, the process must be endothermic.

required_elements: Solubility increases (17.9 M → 19.8 M) with increasing temperature. Le Chatelier: temperature increase favors the endothermic direction → dissolution is endothermic.
alternatives.correct_example: Endothermic. Increasing temperature from 20°C to 25°C increases the solubility of urea (17.9 M → 19.8 M). By Le Chatelier's principle, increasing temperature shifts equilibrium toward the products for an endothermic reaction (the system absorbs the added heat). Since more urea dissolves at higher temperature, the dissolution process absorbs heat and is endothermic.

wrong_examples: Saying exothermic — if dissolution were exothermic, increasing temperature would decrease solubility (Le Chatelier shifts left). The solubility increased, not decreased.
Confusing direction: for exothermic reactions, higher T decreases K; for endothermic reactions, higher T increases K.
common_traps: Le Chatelier and temperature: endothermic reaction → higher T → more product (higher solubility). Exothermic reaction → higher T → less product (lower solubility). Solubility went up with T → endothermic.

parts.prompt:
The equipment shown above is provided so that the student can determine the value of the molar heat of
solution for urea. Knowing that the specific heat of the solution is 4.18 J/(g⋅°C), list the specific
measurements that are required to be made during the experiment.
S° (J/(mol⋅K))
H NCONH (s) 104.6
H NCONH (aq)?
2 2
parts.scoring_notes: To calculate heat of solution ($\Delta H_{soln}$) using $q = mc\Delta T$ and then convert to kJ/mol, the student must measure: (1) mass of water (or solution) in the calorimeter, (2) initial temperature of the water (before adding urea), (3) final temperature of the solution (after urea dissolves), and (4) mass of urea dissolved (to calculate moles).

scoring_points.description: For listing the required measurements: mass of solution, temperature change (initial and final), and mass of urea: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: mass of urea, mass of water, initial temperature of water, final temperature of solution. 1 point is earned for the masses. 1 point is earned for the temperatures.

sample_responses.response: Required measurements: (1) mass of urea dissolved, (2) mass of water in the calorimeter, (3) initial temperature of the water, (4) final temperature of the solution after dissolution.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns both sub-points: identifies both masses (urea and water) and both temperatures (initial and final).

sample_responses.response: Measure the temperature of the solution before and after dissolving the urea.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the masses sub-point: identifies the temperature measurements but omits the masses of urea and water needed for $q = mc\Delta T$ and moles calculation.

required_elements: Mass of water (or solution) to use in $q = mc\Delta T$.
Initial and final temperatures (or ΔT) of the solution.
Mass of urea dissolved (to calculate moles).
alternatives.correct_example:
Required measurements:
(1) Mass of water (solvent) in the calorimeter (g)
(2) Initial temperature of the water (°C)
(3) Final temperature of the solution after urea dissolves (°C)
(4) Mass of urea added to the calorimeter (g)
These allow calculation of $q = mc\Delta T$ (heat absorbed/released) and moles urea = mass/M$_r$, then $\Delta H_{soln}$ = q/moles.

wrong_examples: Listing only temperature change without mass — both are needed for $q = mc\Delta T$.
Forgetting mass of urea — moles of urea must be calculated to express results as kJ/mol.
common_traps: $\Delta H_{soln}$ = q/n. Need q (from $mc\Delta T$) and n (from mass/molar mass). Both mass of solvent AND mass of solute AND temperature change are required measurements.

parts.prompt:
The entropy change for the dissolution of urea, DS , is 70.1 J/(mol⋅K) at 25°C. Using the information in
soln
the table above, calculate the absolute molar entropy, S°, of aqueous urea.

scoring_points.description: For the correct S° of urea(aq) = 174.7 J/(mol·K): 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: $\Delta S°_{soln} = S°(\text{H}_2\text{NCONH}_2(aq)) - S°(\text{H}_2\text{NCONH}_2(s))$; $70.1\text{ J/(mol·K)} = S°(\text{H}_2\text{NCONH}_2(aq)) - 104.6\text{ J/(mol·K)}$; $S°(\text{H}_2\text{NCONH}_2(aq)) = 174.7\text{ J/(mol·K)}$. 1 point is earned for the correct answer.

sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: correctly applies the definition of $\Delta S°$ and solves for the unknown.
Does not earn the point: subtracted in the wrong direction. $\Delta S° = S°_{products} - S°_{reactants}$, so $S°_{product}$ = $\Delta S°$ + $S°_{reactant}$ = 70.1 + 104.6 = 174.7.

wrong_examples: Subtracting instead of adding: $70.1 - 104.6 = -34.5$ — this is not correct; must add ΔS to S° of reactant.
Using the wrong formula for ΔS — ΔS = S$_{products}$ − S$_{reactants}$, so S$_{product}$ = ΔS + S$_{reactant}$.
common_traps: $\Delta S = S°(\text{products}) - S°(\text{reactants})$. For dissolution: urea(s) → urea(aq). $S°(\text{aq}) = \Delta S + S°(\text{s})$.

parts.prompt:
Using particle-level reasoning, explain why DS is positive for the dissolution of urea in water.
soln
parts.scoring_notes: ΔS$_{soln}$ > 0 because the dissolution of solid urea into aqueous solution greatly increases the number of possible arrangements (microstates) of the urea molecules. In the solid, urea molecules are arranged in a regular crystal lattice — low entropy. When dissolved, urea molecules are dispersed randomly throughout the solution, can move and rotate in many more orientations and positions — high entropy. More possible arrangements → higher entropy.

scoring_points.description: For particle-level reasoning: solid → solution increases positions/arrangements available to urea molecules: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: Urea molecules in solution have a greater number of possible arrangements than in solid urea. This increased number of arrangements corresponds to a positive $\Delta S°_{soln}$. 1 point is earned for a correct explanation.

sample_responses.response: In solid urea, molecules are locked in a fixed, ordered crystal lattice with very limited motion. When dissolved, urea molecules are randomly dispersed throughout the solution and have far more possible positions and orientations. This greatly increased number of possible arrangements (microstates) corresponds to a higher entropy → $\Delta S°_{soln} > 0$.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: correctly uses particle-level language (fixed lattice vs. randomly dispersed) to explain the entropy increase.

sample_responses.response: Entropy increases because dissolving is a spontaneous process.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point: does not provide particle-level reasoning. Must explain WHY entropy increases in terms of molecular arrangements or microstates.

required_elements: In the solid, urea molecules are fixed in an ordered crystal lattice with limited motion.
In solution, urea molecules are randomly dispersed and have many more possible positions/orientations/arrangements → greater entropy.
alternatives.correct_example: In solid urea, molecules are held in fixed positions in an ordered crystal lattice, limiting their motion and possible arrangements. When urea dissolves, the molecules separate and are distributed throughout the solution. The dissolved urea molecules have far more possible positions, orientations, and translational/rotational degrees of freedom. More microstates (possible arrangements) means higher entropy in solution → ΔS$_{soln}$ > 0.

wrong_examples: Saying entropy increases because the solid melts — dissolution into solution, not melting into liquid. The key is dispersion of particles throughout the solvent.

Saying entropy decreases because water molecules organize around urea — while water hydration shell ordering decreases entropy slightly, the overall dissolution entropy is positive because urea dispersion dominates.
common_traps: Focus on the urea particles: solid = ordered (low S) → dispersed in solution = disordered (high S). More possible arrangements → ΔS > 0.

parts.prompt:
The student claims that ΔS ° for the process contributes to the thermodynamic favorability of the dissolution
of urea at 25°C. Use the thermodynamic information above to support the student's claim.
parts.scoring_notes: For thermodynamic favorability: $\Delta G° = \Delta H° - T\Delta S°$. ΔS$°_{soln}$ = +70.1 J/(mol·K) > 0. The −TΔS° term = −(298 K)(0.0701 kJ/mol·K) = −20.9 kJ/mol (negative). A negative −TΔS° term makes ΔG° more negative (more favorable). Therefore the positive ΔS° contributes to the thermodynamic favorability (makes ΔG° more negative).

scoring_points.description: For using ΔG° = ΔH° − TΔS° to show that positive ΔS° makes ΔG° more negative (favorable): 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: Thermodynamic favorability for a process at standard conditions is determined by the sign of $\Delta G°$, with $\Delta G° = \Delta H° - T\Delta S°$. Since $\Delta S°$ is positive, the $T\Delta S°$ term makes the value of $\Delta G°$ smaller and thus makes the dissolution more thermodynamically favorable. 1 point is earned for the correct answer.

sample_responses.response: $\Delta G° = \Delta H° - T\Delta S°$. Since $\Delta S° = +70.1\text{ J/(mol·K)} > 0$, the $-T\Delta S°$ term is negative. This negative contribution makes $\Delta G°$ more negative (smaller), which increases thermodynamic favorability. Therefore $\Delta S°$ contributes to making dissolution favorable.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: explicitly uses the Gibbs equation and shows that positive $\Delta S°$ decreases $\Delta G°$.

sample_responses.response: Since $\Delta S° > 0$, the reaction is spontaneous.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point: positive $\Delta S°$ alone does not guarantee spontaneity (ΔH also matters). Must use $\Delta G° = \Delta H° - T\Delta S°$ to demonstrate the contribution.

required_elements: The −TΔS° term is negative at 298 K → contributes negatively to ΔG° → more thermodynamically favorable.
alternatives.correct_example: $\Delta G° = \Delta H° - T\Delta S°$. ΔS° = +70.1 J/(mol·K) = +0.0701 kJ/(mol·K). At T = 298 K: $-T\Delta S° = -(298)(0.0701) = -20.9\text{ kJ/mol}$. This negative contribution to ΔG° makes dissolution more thermodynamically favorable (more negative ΔG°). The student's claim is correct: positive entropy change contributes to favorability.

wrong_examples: Saying ΔS > 0 always makes a reaction spontaneous — ΔG° = ΔH° − TΔS° must be overall negative for spontaneity. ΔS > 0 helps but ΔH also matters.
Not connecting ΔS to ΔG° — must explicitly use the Gibbs equation to support the claim.
common_traps: ΔG° = ΔH° − TΔS°. A positive ΔS° means −TΔS° is negative, which REDUCES ΔG° (makes it more negative). This is the thermodynamic contribution of entropy to favorability.
